summarization.a: This mulitple line plot represents the normalized, integrated absorbance of key IR vibrations (W–F stretch and Si–H stretches) against the total Si₂H₆ exposure during the WF₆ half-reaction at 425 K. It shows the W–F signal decaying and the Si–H signal growing as exposure increases, both saturating at high exposure.

data_extraction.a:
| Si₂H₆ Exposure (Torr min) | Normalized Integrated Absorbance | Stretch |
|---|---|-----|
| 0.0 | 0.0 | W-F Stretch |
| 0.0 | 0.0 | Si-H Stretch |
| 0.1 | 0.04 | W-F Stretch |
| 0.1 | 0.8 | Si-H Stretch |
| 0.2 | 0.007 | W-F Stretch |
| 0.2 | 0.9 | Si-H Stretch |
| 0.3 | -0.01 | W-F Stretch |
| 0.3 | 1 | Si-H Stretch |
| 0.4 | -0.01 | W-F Stretch |
| 0.4 | 1 | Si-H Stretch |
| 0.5 | -0.02 | W-F Stretch |
| 0.5 | 1 | Si-H Stretch |

a.question: What control mechanism does the plateau at exposures >0.3 Torr·min demonstrate?
a.answer: The plateau indicates a self-limiting control mechanism, a hallmark of ALD. Once the reactive Si-H surface sites are fully consumed (absorbance ~ 0) and replaced by W-F species, the reaction stops automatically, meaning extending the exposure time further yields no additional material growth.

a.question: If I consider reducing the WF₆ exposure to 0.1 Torr·min to increase throughput. Compare the surface state at 0.1 Torr·min vs. 0.6 Torr·min and evaluate the trade-off between speed and film quality.
a.answer:
0.1 Torr·min (Speed): High throughput, but the Si-H signal is still ~0.3 (30% unreacted), and W-F coverage is only ~80%.

0.6 Torr·min (Quality): Slower throughput, but Si-H is fully removed (~0.0) and W-F is saturated (1.0).

a.question: Describe the surface composition at the 'crossover point' (~0.05 Torr·min) compared to the final state.
a.answer: At the crossover point, the surface is a heterogeneous mixture of approximately 50% unreacted Si-H bonds and 50% newly formed W-F species.

a.question: The Si-H absorbance drops to near zero at saturation. If this value remained at 0.2 (20%) instead, what would this imply for the resistivity of the final Tungsten interconnect?
a.answer: The saturation value greater than 1.0 indicates a high density of reactive Si–H sites, possibly including higher-order silane species (SiHₓ). Such a hydrogen-rich surface is advantageous because Si–H bonds serve as the reducing agent for WF₆. A larger population of these active sites promotes fast and uniform nucleation of tungsten in the following deposition step.
